speaker: interviewer
text: May 26, 1995-- this is tape two. I'd like to talk about the other woman with her two children that Tmosz was hiding. Did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We met.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was hide in a barn not far where we are hiding. When I-- when I used to see her, her boyfriend, Tmosz, came up to the-- to the place where we were, and he took me to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he introduced you to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just she likes to have company.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was like about 35 years old, two children, very, very pretty, pretty woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had she been hidden with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also the same-- the same time. She was there before we came. But she was hiding also two years with her two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she find out about Tmosz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband used to deal with Tmosz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think Tmosz hid her? Why did he hide her?

speaker: interviewee
text: He hide her because he knew very good her husband, and he-- he work for him. He worked for him. So-- and he-- he is a very good person. Like we didn't know him at all, and he hide us. So he did it for him. He took away the-- his wife and the two children from Kowel, and he gave her a place in his barn. Also, she was-- she had the good straw. He made a place for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have better conditions than you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh a lot better. You cannot compare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they lived like man and wife. She was her-- he likes her, and she had everything. She was the-- she was the [NON-ENGLISH] for the house. But she said he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Tmosz married? Was he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was married. His wife was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But yet he had a relationship with this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't-- she didn't say anything. She was also very good. She knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he expect any favors from you or your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you had lesser conditions as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she has everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her condition?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our condition was we didn't have nothing to eat. We-- I-- we couldn't-- we didn't have where to wash. We didn't wash ourselves-- never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: We been hiding downstairs where the-- the cows and the horses. He had two horses. And upstairs we had a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was in the barn. The barn was big, nice. Yeah. And the roof, it was a-- we had a-- it was a hole. He didn't fix it. And when the-- the-- the snow came in, it came in on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have blankets?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have anything-- just my coat and my sister's coat. This was our blankets. But it was one winter, it was very cold, the second winter. And-- and he had the horses. He had a-- a-- a special blanket for the horses. So he took off from the horses the blanket then gave it to us. It was so cold when we wake up. So we have to take away the snow from the-- from the faces. The eyes were buried with snow-- cold. It was a very bad winter. With--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other effects from the weather conditions on you or your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My sister got sick, and she had rheumatic fever. That's why she was sick with a heart condition. Yeah. And I-- my hair went out. I lost my hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost them. All my hair I lost. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily activity like? Were you able to move around?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I moved, my-- my brother-in-law have to turn on the other way. And when my sister moved, I have to turn the other way. We couldn't sleep. Like, one has to sleep on a side. It was a very, very tiny place. We have to live like this. We survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were able to walk around at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't walk, because when we walked, so-- with the-- the-- the place with the-- somebody would see it something. We couldn't walk. We couldn't talk even. I never talk loud. I talked like I'm talking now. Because the voices were low. We couldn't talk loud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe for me a typical day. The sun came up. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked to each other. We-- we are talking about bread and get a little-- little more piece bread. A little piece of bread would be nice. We just talk about something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lay up in the rafters of the barn all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen if you needed to go to the washroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother-in-law went down. He took the-- the thing down, and he put in the-- in the place where the cows are. He put it down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, you spent 24 hours--

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 Hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: --lying down in these rafters?

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 months we spent in this place. And we've been lucky we spend there, because no one didn't have a place like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did Tmosz or his daughters come see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes when they-- when they knew we are very hungry. They brought us-- at night-- not the day time. At night, they brought us something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would bring food on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not every day-- from time to time. It was very hard. We've been very hungry everyday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going? What sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we speak. We-- we cried. We-- we said maybe we won't-- we won't live. We didn't expected to live. But we live day by day-- maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any days different from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing-- every day was the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was aware that you were hiding in the barn? Was it just Tmosz and his daughters, or were there other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, their daughters, and it was a little boy. He went with the cows every day. He took the cows out. But he knew we are-- we are hiding, but he didn't say anything. He knew about that, but he didn't say anything. I never saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times during these two years would you see this other lady with her two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes when Tmosz has to go away, like he went somewhere to buy something for a day, so she asked him to bring me. So I came there. And she had bread and everything. So she gave me to eat. So I didn't finish. I didn't eat everything. I just saved for my sister and brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Essentially then, for two years, every day you spent with your sister and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motl Fisher who was later to become your brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. And sometimes we didn't have nothing to eat. And Ola, the older sister, came up to us. So I said to her, you know what? You have a white linen. They made by themselves the white linen. Give me the white linen. They were poor also. Give me the white linen, and I'll make you a dress. You know, I'll make you nice in the front. We made it. If you have a needle and a-- and a scissor-- and I'll make you something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't do-- I didn't know what to do. So I-- I had my dress. I took off my dress. I took off everything, and I made according to my dress. I made her a dress and her sister a dress. She was very happy. Then she brought us bread-- something to eat. We've been very happy. Was this towards the end of the two years or was this in the very beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- was the beginning that-- it was every day was the same. We've been used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your body react to the lack of movement, and exercise, and the lack of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, nothing. We didn't-- we were-- we been very skinny, both of us, me and my sister. My sister was very sick. We couldn't do anything. This break my heart. We went-- we went out when we are-- went to a doctor. So he said, your heart is finished. We can't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no medical facility available?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tmosz couldn't bring anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And we couldn't go to a doctor. It was very hard for us. The one-- one thing-- it was-- it's a shame to tell. But when we have our period-- so we had two little schmattas-- two little one. And we need it. So-- when they were with blood-- so we have to piss on them to make them clean. And we made them-- and we put them under our-- to make them dry. We dried them. Oh, it was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd sit on them to dry them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No water-- nothing. And the hardest day, you need a little bit of water-- no way. We've been very hungry. One night when they gave the-- the cows and the-- and the-- to eat, they-- they gave potatoes. And from the-- from the straw they made-- ah, what to say-- from the-- they made--

speaker: interviewer
text: The feed for the animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The feed for the animals. They made with the potatoes. And when they-- when they left, my brother-in-law went down, and we took a little bit of the-- those food from the cows. And we had to eat. We had this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were you able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often. When-- we-- we've been very hungry. We hadn't got nothing to eat. He gave us a-- a little bread for-- for three of us. So I can-- it wasn't enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see an end in sight, or every day was just another day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyday was the same. We been thinking maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, we'll-- we'll survive. Everyday was the same. We survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about for those two years other than surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. We couldn't think anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk? Did you sing? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked. Yeah. We talked. We saw from the-- from the-- it was-- we saw outside like cats and-- and dogs. So my sister said, I would-- I will like to be a cat. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I would like to be a dog-- it's better-- better for them. It was better for them than for us. What is your most vivid memory of those two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: The memory-- everyday was the same. I see the same thing everyday. When I talk to you, I see everything. I see the place where we-- where we hide. And I-- I see the-- the roof. I see the straw. And I see the bad times we had. We can't describe what we got through there. It's nothing to-- I can't tell you what we got through. But we can't forget. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When did this come to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- we've been free 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came up, and he said, you know what? Tmosz came up. You're free. So we said, but how? What? We can't believe it. It's not true. So we start to cry. So he said, you know what? They kill all the Jews. But I am going to keep us for-- for a siege. That's what he said. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What happened that day that he told you you were free? What was the first thing you thought of?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't want to go out. We been afraid to go out. We stayed a day more.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the rafters?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the place. Because we didn't believe that we are free. So he came in in the morning. Like it was about 10 o'clock in the morning. He said, we are there? He wants to know, because we didn't go. We didn't say goodbye.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yeah, we are still there. So he said, you're crazy! You're free! So I said, that's true, Tmosz? He said, yes. So we came down. And we couldn't walk. We couldn't walk on our feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a-- so we start to walk, and we couldn't. And-- and my sister start to walk. And she said, I'm walking. Let's walk. Don't say you can't walk. We start to walk, and we walked. But it was very hard. Two years we've been sitting in one place. So we couldn't walk, and we couldn't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, it comes. Every day was better. We walked. We didn't know where to walk. Where should we walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish woman with her two children she-- he told her to stay there, to remain with them. And to us, he said, you have to go to find something. So we went. And we walked, maybe one-- one week. We-- we walked every day, every place we find.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you walking to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where. But we came to one place. We came into a house. It was Ukrainian. And she took us in. And she said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know the people. She took us in, and she said, I will make you potatoes. Don't worry. You can sleep over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in the house, it was a-- it was-- he was a church man. He also came, because all the people were running after the war. So he said, you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, yes. We just-- so he said to her, don't make them potatoes or nothing. Tell them to go. So she was afraid, and she didn't give us nothing. And she told us to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at night. We been hungry, and wet. So we went away. And we start to cry, you see? We are free. But this is free? They didn't let us stay. So we walked that night. It was cold. So we say-- we-- we saw from far away a house. We went into the house. It was-- it start to be day-- daytime. It was about 4 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the house. No one was there. And we find two potatoes-- two raw potatoes. So we eat the-- the two raw potatoes-- the three of us. My-- my brother-in-law, I gave him one potato and we had two, me and my sister. And we saw a place where to hide, to sleepover. So we came into the place. And my-- and my brother-in-law said, you stay here. And I'm going to see where I am. And he came out. And he thought-- he-- he start to walk. He walked maybe 1/2 a mile. Two-- two men stopped him. It was the Russian people-- the Russian soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't see them. They-- they came in. You know? And they took it. Then he-- he said from-- to us, don't worry. Don't worry. They won't kill us. Be free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to a place where the soldiers are. They gave us something to eat. And we told them to take us somewhere where maybe they know where Jewish people are alive. They took us to a little place. And there [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- the name of the place-- I remember now-- [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a little town. And we went to this town. And there we met-- we met Jewish people in a house. It-- it's a-- it's born a family. They came back, and they find the house-- their house. So everyone who was free came to this house. So it was maybe 30 Jewish people that came together. We stayed there, maybe a-- a week. They gave us to eat-- one-- who has something to eat, he gave us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't know each other, but one said I'm from this place. I'm from that place. And we've been happy. We've been very close all together. We cried together. We've been happy together. And we stayed just one week. We couldn't stay more, because there were no place. We-- we slept in a-- in a chair-- where we find. And then we went to see-- to see where-- to see a place where to live, where to stay. So we walked another week. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were walking, were there other people walking as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. We-- we find-- we find two sisters and a brother. They're from-- from-- [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. So we got very close, and we walked. We didn't know where we walk. But we walked to see people, to see a city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people? Yeah. They live in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Her name was Gittel and Tova. And her brother was Avrum. So we walk together until we came to Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there we-- we find one man. He was Jewish, so we ask him, where is a place to live? There is some Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go in into this house. It's a lady. She will-- she will give you a place. And she will give you eat-- something to eat. So when I came in, I saw my-- my mother's cousin-- my mother's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Faigy-- [NON-ENGLISH] Faigy, yeah-- her-- the same name like my mother-- Sadler-- Faigy Sadler. And she didn't let us go. She gave us bread and everything. And then we-- we start to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- we been crying-- both of us. She start to cry. She was very happy to see us-- me and my sister. And she took us all in her house. We slept over a few nights. And she gave us bread and everything. And then we-- we've been looking for another place to live. We find a place. And we live all together-- the two sisters, and the brother, and me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maniewicze. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find another place to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We find a house, a empty house. Yeah. And we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people in the town who were willing to help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. No one help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I washed the-- the-- from the-- from the Russian soldiers. I washed their-- their clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In exchange for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then-- and then it was very hard for me to do that. So, when we had a place-- so one-- so-- so one woman said she has a machine-- a thing-- a sewing machine. So I said, you know what? You lend me the machine. And I'm going to start to-- to do something. If you have to fix something, I'll will do for you. I was very handy always.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, OK. If you'll pay me three hundred ruble a week, I'll give you the machine. So I said to my sister, where do I-- I-- I give the-- I have no money. So she said, you know what? Take it. If you-- if you don't have the money, you'll give it back. She won't kill you. So I took the machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other day-- the Russian people, they get the-- the-- the blouses. They had the green blouses. They had without-- without the pockets. So when they got them, they-- they made-- somebody made for them pockets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one soldier came in, and he said, can you make me a pocket? So I said, yes, if you bring me an old one. And I'll take off everything-- the-- the pieces. I'll do-- I'll do the same. I will make you. And he brought me a old one. I took off everything, and I make him the plackets. So every day another one came in, and he gave me the work. So I was very good in them. I learn myself how to do. So I made the pockets. And they brought me food. So we had something to eat. And I gave to other people-- to the two sisters and brother. We lived together, and we had together everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How long did that go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that go-- go down for maybe three months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day another soldier came in, and he said he's a-- he-- he is a tailor. He makes-- he can make hats. He makes hats. So, he had-- I do the hats. And-- and there-- there were people, they need hats. So he-- he-- he show me out to do it, and I did. He helped me. And I made the-- I-- I made money to live. This is the way I did. It was very hard. But I try, and I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the blouses-- every time another man came, another soldier came. And one of the soldiers had to-- came in. He was an officer. And he said to me, you tell me your whole story-- how it was. And he was a writer. Now he start-- everyday he came in, and I told him our life story. And then the war break out again. The German came back. We have to run. And-- and he got killed. They told me. One soldier told me he got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there we went to-- they took away-- I went to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, good. Oh, I'm happy. [LAUGHS]
